title: How to Focus on Stars at Night
excerpt: Getting tack-sharp stars is the #1 struggle for night photographers. Here are five proven methods that work every time, even in pitch darkness.
author: Ace Stellar
body:
## Why Your Stars Are Blurry

You drove an hour to a dark sky site, set up in the cold, nailed your composition, dialed in perfect exposure settings, and... every star is a soft blob. Your entire night of shooting is ruined by one thing: bad focus.

This is the single most common astrophotography frustration, and it's completely preventable once you learn the right techniques. Let's fix this forever.

## Why Autofocus Fails at Night

Your camera's autofocus system needs contrast to lock on. Stars are tiny pinpoints of light against a black background — there simply isn't enough contrast for most AF systems to grab onto. Even cameras with excellent low-light AF will hunt endlessly, racking the focus back and forth without ever locking.

Some newer mirrorless cameras (Sony a7IV, Canon R6 II) can sometimes autofocus on very bright stars or the Moon, but it's unreliable. For astrophotography, manual focus is king. Accept it, embrace it, and learn to do it well.

## Method 1: Live View Zoom (The Gold Standard)

This is the method used by 90% of experienced astrophotographers. It works with any camera that has Live View.

**Step by step:**

1. Switch your lens to manual focus (MF).
2. Turn on Live View / LCD screen mode.
3. Point your camera at the brightest star or planet visible. Vega, Sirius, Jupiter, or Arcturus are great targets.
4. Zoom in on the LCD. Most cameras let you zoom 5x or 10x. Go to maximum zoom.
5. You'll see the star as a bloated, out-of-focus blob.
6. Slowly turn the focus ring. Watch the star shrink.
7. Keep turning — the star will get smaller and smaller until it reaches its minimum size (a tight pinpoint).
8. If you go too far, it'll start getting bigger again. Back up.
9. Find the sweet spot where the star is the absolute smallest point of light.
10. Take a test shot, review it at 100% zoom, and verify the stars are sharp dots.

**Pro tips for Live View focusing:**
- Increase your ISO temporarily to 12800+ to make faint stars visible on the LCD. Reset to your shooting ISO after focusing.
- If your camera has focus peaking, turn it on — it highlights in-focus areas.
- Some cameras have a brightness boost mode for Live View (Sony: Bright Monitoring). Use it.
- On mirrorless cameras, the electronic viewfinder (EVF) often shows a better, more magnified view than the rear LCD.

## Method 2: The Bright Star Diffraction Method

This works especially well with lenses that have an aperture diaphragm (not fully wide open).

1. Point at a bright star (magnitude 0-1, like Sirius, Vega, or Arcturus).
2. Stop down your aperture to f/8 or f/11.
3. In Live View, the star will show diffraction spikes (star-shaped points).
4. Adjust focus until the spikes are sharpest and most symmetrical.
5. Open back up to your shooting aperture (f/2.8, f/1.4, etc.).
6. The focus position should remain correct (but take a test shot to verify).

The advantage of this method is that diffraction spikes are easier to judge by eye than a plain pinpoint.

## Method 3: The Bahtinov Mask

A Bahtinov mask is a patterned screen that fits over your lens. It creates a distinctive three-line diffraction pattern on bright stars:

- When out of focus, the middle line is offset from center.
- When perfectly focused, the middle line passes exactly through the intersection of the other two.

**How to use it:**
1. Attach the mask to your lens (they come in various sizes, or you can 3D print one).
2. Point at a bright star.
3. In Live View, you'll see three diffraction lines.
4. Adjust focus until the center line bisects the X pattern perfectly.
5. Remove the mask and shoot.

Bahtinov masks are especially valuable for telescopes, where focus is extremely critical and the depth of field is tiny. For wide-angle lenses, Live View zoom is usually sufficient, but a Bahtinov mask removes all guesswork.

You can buy them for $10-20 or print one for free (search "Bahtinov mask generator").

## Method 4: The Tape-It-Down Trick

Once you've achieved perfect focus using any method above, the nightmare scenario is accidentally bumping the focus ring and losing it. The solution is elegantly low-tech:

1. Achieve perfect focus.
2. Take a piece of gaffer tape (not masking tape — it leaves residue).
3. Tape the focus ring to the lens barrel so it can't rotate.
4. Optionally, mark the position with a paint pen or white marker.

Now you can bump the lens, change batteries, even pack up and set up again — and your focus stays locked.

**Even better:** Some lenses have a focus distance scale. Note the exact position for infinity focus on your specific lens (it's often not at the infinity symbol). Next time, you can dial it in before even going outside.

## Method 5: Daytime Pre-Focus

Here's a clever shortcut:

1. During the day, focus on a very distant object (a mountain, a cell tower miles away, a distant building on the horizon).
2. At astrophotography distances, anything beyond about half a mile is effectively at infinity.
3. Mark the focus position on your lens with tape or a marker.
4. At night, just dial to that mark.

This isn't quite as precise as live-star focusing because temperature changes can slightly shift focus (thermal expansion of the lens), but it gets you very close. You can then fine-tune with Live View.

## The Infinity Focus Myth

New astrophotographers often think they should just twist the focus ring to the infinity symbol and they're done. Unfortunately:

- Most modern lenses focus slightly past infinity. The hard stop isn't true infinity.
- The infinity mark on the lens is approximate, not precise.
- Temperature affects focus position (glass elements expand and contract).
- Zoom lenses shift focus when you change focal length.

Never trust the infinity mark. Always verify with Live View or a test shot.

## Checking Your Focus (The Critical Step Most People Skip)

After setting focus with any method:

1. Take a test exposure at your planned shooting settings.
2. Review the image on your camera's LCD.
3. Zoom in to 100% (pixel level) on a star near the center AND corners.
4. Stars should be tight pinpoints. If they look like donuts, discs, or blobs, refocus.
5. Corner stars will often be softer than center stars due to lens aberrations — that's normal, especially at wide apertures.

**Do this check every 30-60 minutes during your shoot.** Temperature changes, accidental bumps, and even gravity (if your lens is pointing up or down) can shift focus over time.

## Focus Troubleshooting

- **Stars are donuts (bright ring with dark center):** You've focused past infinity. Pull back.
- **Stars look like comets (teardrop shape):** This is coma, a lens aberration. It's worse in corners and at wide apertures. Stopping down to f/2.8-4 helps, or use a coma corrector.
- **Stars are sharp in center but blurry in corners:** Normal lens behavior, especially at wide apertures. Stop down one stop for better corners.
- **Focus keeps shifting:** Temperature is changing. Check and refine every 30-60 minutes.
- **Can't see any stars in Live View:** Boost ISO to maximum, point at the brightest star/planet available, and make sure you're in a dark area (not standing under a street light).

## The Bottom Line

Perfect focus is the difference between a stunning astrophoto and a blurry mess. It takes 2-3 minutes to do right. Spend those minutes every single session. Your future self, reviewing razor-sharp stars on a big monitor, will thank you.

One more thing: if your focus was slightly off and you got a bunch of soft shots... don't delete them. Processing techniques like deconvolution can recover some sharpness, and stacking many slightly-soft frames produces better results than you'd expect. But always aim for sharp from the start.
